extracted_information: The web content provides a comprehensive overview of database scaling patterns, focusing on horizontal scaling techniques like replication (read replicas), partitioning (sharding), and caching. It distinguishes between vertical and horizontal scaling, emphasizing horizontal scaling for distributed systems. Detailed explanations are given for each method, including their purpose, mechanisms, and challenges.

features.name: Vertical Scaling
features.description: Upgrading a single server by adding more resources (CPU, memory, disk space) to increase its capacity. It is the easiest and quickest way to scale but has limitations in cost and available technology, leading to a limit on how much a database can be vertically scaled.

features.name: Horizontal Scaling
features.description: Connecting multiple computers (machines or nodes) to share the workload, forming a 'distributed system'. It is more flexible and cost-effective than vertical scaling and is almost always used in system design due to vertical scaling's limitations.
features.terminology: Machines, nodes are interchangeable terms referring to individual computers in a distributed system.

features.name: Read Scaling with Replication
features.description: A technique to scale read operations by creating additional copies of data (replicas) across multiple machines. Clients can read from these copies, distributing the read load and managing increasing read requests.

details.write_flow: Client writes to the primary node, which asynchronously passes updates to read replicas.
details.read_flow: Clients can read from any replica.
details.other_methods_mentioned: Multi-leader replication, Leaderless replication (covered in a separate section).
benefits: Redundancy: Multiple copies of data ensure service continuation if one machine fails.
High Availability: Service remains operational even with machine breakdowns.

features.name: Read Scaling with Caching
features.description: Storing frequently accessed data in a faster storage medium (cache) for quicker retrieval. The goal is for the cache to handle most requests, accessing the database only when data is not in the cache.
features.details: A simple concept with many considerations to be covered in a separate caching section.

features.name: Write Scaling with Partitioning (Sharding)
features.description: A method of horizontally partitioning a database table by spreading data across multiple servers. Each partition (shard) shares the same schema but contains unique data. This distributes the write load across partitions.

details.purpose: To store more data by adding more machines and to distribute write load.
details.mechanism: A write request goes to only one partition, distributing the load.
details.partition_key: The field used to decide which partition a piece of data goes to, aiming for even distribution (e.g., MOD of user_id).
challenges: Performing range queries: Difficult with basic hash partitioning for fields requiring counting and range queries.
Hot partitions: Uneven traffic distribution if a partition key leads to disproportionately high activity on one shard (e.g., celebrity user).
Rebalancing: Adding new partitions requires changing the hashing function and moving data across existing partitions.

features.name: Relationship Between Partitioning and Replication
features.description: These two techniques are often used together to provide fault tolerance. Copies of each partition are stored on multiple nodes, meaning each record exists in only one partition but may live on multiple nodes. Each node can act as a leader for some partitions and a follower for others. If a node fails, the system continues to operate because other nodes with replicas of the affected partition can serve requests.
